positional_bias.note: Sidecar uses 1-based keys {1:1, 2:2, 3:1, 4:2}; GIFT-derived 0-based positions {0:1, 1:2, 2:1, 3:2}. Values match after offset normalisation. All four positions used.

length_bias.finding: Cycle 1 fix confirmed. In all 6 questions correct answers are shorter than distractors. Correct answers are now the shortest option in every question (rate 0.00). Length bias eliminated.

weakest_questions.distractor: Deepfake tools were banned globally in 2019 after governments determined they posed an immediate, unmanageable risk to national security and financial systems.
weakest_questions.note: Not a common beginner misconception; factually implausible but corpus explicitly refutes it (§5.4 notes tools became easier to use, not banned). Not absurd — addresses a real claim students might have encountered in news framing.

weakest_questions.distractor: Bias only matters when the AI operates in countries that have passed anti-discrimination laws specifically covering algorithmic decision-making.
weakest_questions.note: Draws on a real policy fact (some jurisdictions have passed such laws) but frames it as the sole condition for bias to matter — a stretch misconception rather than a common one.

weakest_questions.distractor: All three involved AI systems deliberately programmed to cause harm by their creators, who encoded discriminatory rules or deceptive capabilities on purpose.
weakest_questions.note: Intent-attribution misconception addressed explicitly in corpus §5.5 Pattern 2. Plausible as a prior belief but stated in extreme form.

weakest_questions.distractor: All three cases were resolved quickly once the AI companies involved issued public apologies and corrections, demonstrating that transparency alone is sufficient to address AI harm.
weakest_questions.note: Weakest distractor in q6 — not a documented common misconception, though it touches a real debate about transparency as a remedy.

los_from_corpus: LO1: Describe at least two documented cases of AI failure in healthcare, hiring, and deepfakes, and explain the real harm each caused.
LO2: Identify the specific mechanism — data problem, design flaw, or misuse — that led to each failure.
LO3: Explain what 'AI bias' means using a concrete example, without falling back on vague language.
LO4: Distinguish between an AI system that is technically working and one that is causing harm.
LO5: Explain why the people and organizations that deploy an AI system bear responsibility for its outcomes, even when the AI produces the output.
objective_coverage.note: q6 maps to §5.5 integrative common-thread intent declared in the sidecar. All 5 LOs covered 1:1. No orphan questions.

scope_discipline.note: All stems use plain language grounded in corpus content. 'Algorithmic bias' (q3 stem implicit), 'diagnostic AI' (q4), 'accountability' (q5) — all defined in the corpus before they appear in quiz options. No distractor requires knowledge outside this topic. No scope creep detected.

auto_correction_applied.note: No auto-correction needed this cycle. Positional bias was within threshold; length bias fix was applied by the builder between cycles.

verdict: Pass — all checks clean. Length bias fixed (rate 0.00, was 1.00 in cycle 1). Positional bias 0.333 ≤ 0.40. Distractor plausibility absurd_rate 0.00. All 5 LOs covered. No scope creep, no jargon in stems, no out-of-scope questions.
